_note: Original Practice questions derived from CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

python.explanation: `len(word)` is 5, so this asks for `word[5]`. The valid indexes are 0 through 4, so there is no character at 5 and Python raises an IndexError. Picking "s" is the tempting answer, because `s` really is the last character. It just lives at index 4, not 5. To get the last character safely, write `word[len(word) - 1]`, or use the shortcut on the next screen.

java.prompt: What happens when Java asks for `charAt(word.length())`?
java.explanation: `word.length()` is 5, so this asks for `word.charAt(5)`. The legal indexes stop at 4, so nothing lives at 5 and the program halts on that line with a StringIndexOutOfBoundsException. "It prints s" is the answer that pulls at you, and for a fair reason: the letter s does sit at the end of the word. Its index is 4, though, not 5, because the count opened at 0. Reach it with `word.charAt(word.length() - 1)`. The code does compile, since the compiler has no way to know what that number will be before the program runs.

javascript.explanation: Slice includes positions zero through two and excludes position three.

cpp.explanation: The string contains three characters, so its size is three.

python.code:
s = "  hello world  "
parts = s.strip().split()
print(len(parts), parts[-1].upper())
python.explanation: Take it one step at a time. `.strip()` trims the spaces on both ends, leaving `"hello world"`. `.split()` then breaks that at the space and gives you the list `['hello', 'world']`, which has 2 items, so `len(parts)` is 2. `parts[-1]` is the last item, `'world'`, and `.upper()` turns it into `'WORLD'`. If you picked 16, you measured the length of the string instead of the list: `len()` counts characters in a string but items in a list.

java.explanation: `b` is built at runtime by joining `a` with "202", so it is a brand new object that happens to hold the same characters as the "cs202" written in the code. `==` compares the two objects, sees that they are not the same one, and prints false. `.equals()` compares the characters, finds them identical, and prints true. If you picked "true then true", you read `==` as a question about the text. It is a question about the object, and that gap is exactly where the bug lives.

javascript.explanation: The method returns a new string without changing the value in label.

cpp.explanation: The call starts at index one and takes two characters, producing od.

python.prompt: Which statement best describes how Python strings work?
choices: Text you can measure, cut apart, and reshape.
It is used only to print a message.
It automatically fixes every program error.
It removes the need for variables and tests.
python.explanation: A string is text in quotes: `"Ada"` or `'Ada'`. `len(s)` tells you how many characters it holds. You reach a single character with `s[i]`, and counting starts at 0, so the last character of a string of length 5 is `s[4]`. `s[-1]` is a shortcut for that last one. `s[1:4]` is a slice: it takes characters 1, 2, and 3, stopping before 4. A string cannot be changed in place, so build a new one instead.

java.prompt: Which statement best describes how Java strings work?
choices: Text is an object in Java, and comparing two of them has one right way.
It is used only to print a message.
It automatically fixes every program error.
It removes the need for variables and tests.
java.explanation: A `String` holds text in double quotes: `"Ada"`. It is an object, not a primitive, so it carries methods you call with a dot: `s.length()`, `s.charAt(0)`, `s.substring(1, 4)`, `s.toUpperCase()`, `s.trim()`, `s.split(",")`. Indexes start at 0, so the last character is at `s.length() - 1`. Strings are immutable, so every method returns a new String and changes nothing. Compare two Strings with `a.equals(b)`. Using `==` compares whether they are the same object in memory, which is not the question you meant to ask.

javascript.prompt: Which statement best describes how JavaScript strings work?
choices: Read and transform text without changing the original value.
It is used only to print a message.
It automatically fixes every program error.
It removes the need for variables and tests.
javascript.explanation: Strings are immutable sequences with zero-based indexes. `.length` counts characters, and template literals insert expressions with `${...}`.

cpp.prompt: Which statement best describes how C++ strings work?
choices: Create, index, compare, and combine text.
It is used only to print a message.
It automatically fixes every program error.
It removes the need for variables and tests.
cpp.explanation: `std::string` stores text, `.size()` reports its character count, and indexing starts at zero. Strings support comparison and concatenation with compatible operands.

python.prompt: What happens when you run `s[0] = "b"` on a string `s = "cat"`?
choices: A TypeError — strings do not support item assignment
python.explanation: Strings are immutable, so assigning into one raises a TypeError: 'str' object does not support item assignment. Reading `s[0]` works, which is the trap. To change a character, build a new string, as in `"b" + s[1:]`, which gives "bat".

java.prompt: What does this print, given `answer` was built as `"ye" + "s"`?
choices: no match — == compares object identity, not characters
java.explanation: A string built at run time is a new object, so `==` (which asks whether two references point at the same object) is false, and this prints "no match". To compare characters use `.equals()`. The code compiles and runs; it just answers the wrong question.

choices: "cs" — toUpperCase returns a new string and the result was not kept
"CS" — the string is changed in place
javascript.explanation: Strings are immutable, so `toUpperCase()` returns a new "CS" and leaves `major` alone. Calling it without assigning throws the result away, so `major` is still "cs". You have to capture it: `major = major.toUpperCase()`.

choices: back() on an empty string is undefined behavior
empty() is the only way to get a string's length
back() returns the first character, not the last
cpp.explanation: Calling `back()` (or reading `text[text.size() - 1]`) on an empty string reads out of bounds, which is undefined behavior. The `empty()` check makes sure there is a last character before you ask for it. `back()` returns the final character, and the guard is about safety, not speed.

python.title: Strings concept check 1
python.prompt: What does the lesson mean by "You cannot add a number to a string"?
choices: `"Score: " + 10` raises a TypeError, because plus means one thing for text and something else for numbers, and Python refuses to guess which you meant. Two ways out.
python.explanation: A string is text in quotes: `"Ada"` or `'Ada'`. `len(s)` tells you how many characters it holds. That reasoning supports the answer: `"Score: " + 10` raises a TypeError, because plus means one thing for text and something else for numbers, and Python refuses to guess which you meant. Two ways out.

java.title: Strings concept check 1
java.prompt: What does the lesson mean by "Java catches type errors before it runs anything"?
choices: Java checks the types of everything while it compiles, which is before a single line of your program has run. So `int n = "hello";` never gets the chance to misbehave.
java.explanation: A `String` holds text in double quotes: `"Ada"`. It is an object, not a primitive, so it carries methods you call with a dot: `s.length()`, `s.charAt(0)`, `s.substring(1, 4)`, `s.toUpperCase()`, `s.trim()`, `s.split(",")`. That reasoning supports the answer: Java checks the types of everything while it compiles, which is before a single line of your program has run. So `int n = "hello";` never gets the chance to misbehave.

javascript.title: Strings concept check 1
javascript.prompt: What does the lesson mean by "Watch this detail"?
choices: A string with length five ends at index four. Reading `text[text.length]` goes one place too far.
javascript.explanation: Strings are immutable sequences with zero-based indexes. `.length` counts characters, and template literals insert expressions with `${...}`. That reasoning supports the answer: A string with length five ends at index four. Reading `text[text.length]` goes one place too far.

cpp.title: Strings concept check 1
cpp.prompt: What does the lesson mean by "C++ detail to check"?
choices: For a nonempty string, the final character is at `text.size() - 1`; using `text[text.size()]` steps past the valid range.
cpp.explanation: `std::string` stores text, `.size()` reports its character count, and indexing starts at zero. Strings support comparison and concatenation with compatible operands. That reasoning supports the answer: For a nonempty string, the final character is at `text.size() - 1`; using `text[text.size()]` steps past the valid range.

python.title: Strings concept check 2
python.prompt: What does the lesson mean by "IndexError: string index out of range"?
choices: `"Bears"` has five characters, so the valid indexes are 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4. Asking for `word[5]` asks for a sixth character that was never there, and Python stops the program rather than invent one.
python.explanation: A string is text in quotes: `"Ada"` or `'Ada'`. `len(s)` tells you how many characters it holds. That reasoning supports the answer: `"Bears"` has five characters, so the valid indexes are 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4. Asking for `word[5]` asks for a sixth character that was never there, and Python stops the program rather than invent one.

choices: "Bears" has five characters, so the legal indexes are 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4. There is no index 5.
java.explanation: A `String` holds text in double quotes: `"Ada"`. It is an object, not a primitive, so it carries methods you call with a dot: `s.length()`, `s.charAt(0)`, `s.substring(1, 4)`, `s.toUpperCase()`, `s.trim()`, `s.split(",")`. That reasoning supports the answer: "Bears" has five characters, so the legal indexes are 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4. There is no index 5.

javascript.title: Strings concept check 2
javascript.prompt: Which choice describes a reliable Strings practice?
javascript.explanation: Strings are immutable sequences with zero-based indexes. `.length` counts characters, and template literals insert expressions with `${...}`. That reasoning supports the answer: Count from index zero.

cpp.title: Strings concept check 2
cpp.prompt: Which choice describes a reliable Strings practice?
cpp.explanation: `std::string` stores text, `.size()` reports its character count, and indexing starts at zero. Strings support comparison and concatenation with compatible operands. That reasoning supports the answer: Include the string header.

choices: This is the bug that costs Java beginners the most time, and the reason is that `==` sometimes appears to work. Compare two strings you typed directly into your code and Java may reuse one object for both, so `==` returns true and you walk away believing it.
java.explanation: A `String` holds text in double quotes: `"Ada"`. It is an object, not a primitive, so it carries methods you call with a dot: `s.length()`, `s.charAt(0)`, `s.substring(1, 4)`, `s.toUpperCase()`, `s.trim()`, `s.split(",")`. That reasoning supports the answer: This is the bug that costs Java beginners the most time, and the reason is that `==` sometimes appears to work. Compare two strings you typed directly into your code and Java may reuse one object for both, so `==` returns true and you walk away believing it.
